reasoning: The finding correctly identifies that line 41 uses current_user_can('administrator') — a role-name string — instead of a capability like manage_options. This is a WordPress best-practice violation in principle. However, the finding does not constitute a real exploitable vulnerability for three reasons: (1) Nonce self-gates the attack: the only nonce-issuance path (mtnc_load_later_scripts, line 239-240) is itself gated by the same current_user_can('administrator') check, so any user who could pass line 41's role check already passed the equivalent check to obtain the nonce; a user who lacks the 'administrator' role label cannot obtain a valid nonce and will fail check_ajax_referer at line 39 first. (2) The AJAX action is registered only as wp_ajax_mtnc_dismiss_notice (authenticated users only, no nopriv handler), so unauthenticated users have no attack surface at all; the reachability claim 'any authenticated user' is factually incorrect. (3) Even if the role-string check were bypassed, the only effect is setting maintenance_meta['hide_welcome_pointer'] = true — dismissing an admin UI welcome pointer tooltip. There is no security-relevant consequence: no privilege escalation, no sensitive data exposed, no attacker-controlled content persisted. The issue is a code quality / best-practice concern, not an exploitable vulnerability.
